main_summary.summary: Mildred Pitts Walter, children's book author and CORE activist, was interviewed by David Cline on March 1, 2013, in San Mateo, California, for the Civil Rights History Project. Born in 1922 in De Ridder, Louisiana, Walter attended a Beauregard Parish all-Black high school where the only new book she owned was Carter G. Woodson's history of the Negro. She worked her way through Southern University, including a wartime stint cleaning ships in Longview, Washington, and graduated in 1944. Moving to Los Angeles, she joined CORE through her husband Earl Walter and participated in pickets of segregated grocery stores and banks along Central Avenue, motel-discrimination test cases enforcing the 1959 Unruh Civil Rights Act, and housing-tract demonstrations in what is now Carson, California, where Nazi-uniformed counter-protesters confronted the line. Encouraged by a Los Angeles publisher, she wrote Lillie of Watts and went on to a writing career producing children's books that earned the Coretta Scott King Award (for Justin and the Best Biscuits in the World), the Jane Addams Children's Book Award, and the Christopher Award. She traveled to FESTAC '77 in Nigeria as the only children's book writer in the U.S. delegation and visited Elmina Castle in Ghana.
key_themes: CORE direct action in Los Angeles
main_summary.historical_significance:
Walter's testimony documents West Coast civil rights organizing that is undercovered in the standard Southern-movement narrative: CORE's 1958-1960s campaigns against segregated employment at Central Avenue grocery stores and banks, the test-case enforcement of the 1959 Unruh Civil Rights Act through coordinated Black-white couple motel applications, and the housing-tract pickets in present-day Carson where American Nazis appeared in uniform with swastika placards. Her career as a children's book author also documents a parallel cultural front of the movement: the deliberate insertion of Black protagonists and Black history into elementary-school classrooms at a time when, by her count, fewer than one percent of children's books were by and for Black children.

The interview is additionally valuable for Walter's account of her 1977 participation in FESTAC (the Second World Black and African Festival of Arts and Culture) in Lagos as the only children's book writer in the U.S. delegation, her subsequent visit to Elmina Castle in Ghana, and her reflection on diaspora identity ("I am not African and I am not American"). Her contemporaneous reference to the Kerner Commission, the Greensboro Truth and Reconciliation Commission (2004-06), and her Soviet Union peace march anchor a long arc of post-1965 civil rights work that connects domestic anti-racism to international peace organizing.

chapters.title: The Only New Book Was Carter G. Woodson
chapters.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in San Mateo, California, on March 1, 2013, Mildred Pitts Walter opens with her birth in Louisiana in 1922, her father's work with the Long-Bell Lumber Company, and growing up in De Ridder between Lake Charles and Shreveport, at a parish high school where the only new book she ever had was Carter G. Woodson's history of the Negro while every other book had pages torn out.

chapters.title: Humility Could Not Be Humiliated
chapters.summary: Walter describes her all-Black school, the new building with electric light wired by a capable principal, the African American teachers who taught the children they were not inferior, and the church and ministers who insisted they hold their heads high, do everything with excellence, and meet those who hated them with a humility that could not be humiliated.

chapters.title: She Insisted I Sit and Eat
chapters.summary: She recounts working from the age of seven, minding a white child, cleaning a beauty shop and homes while attending high school, and a woman from Ohio whose husband was a soldier at the nearby camp during World War II, who hired her, insisted she sit at the table and eat, gave her magazines to discuss, and said she was the only person there she felt comfortable with.

chapters.title: To Earn Enough to Finish Southern
chapters.topic: Southern University and the War North
chapters.summary: Walter recalls attending Southern University in Scotlandville, Louisiana, where leaders such as Mordecai Wyatt Johnson and W.E.B. Du Bois came to inspire the students, and how in her senior year she followed a sister to Longview, Washington, to work in the shipyards and earn enough money to finish Southern.

chapters.title: They Laughed at My Application
chapters.summary: She describes applying for a personnel job at the shipyards with experience working under the dean of women, only to be laughed at amid wartime discrimination, ending up cleaning ships and working in gyro rooms, and waking her exhausted Black and white coworkers on the graveyard shift before the supervisor arrived.

chapters.title: All the Water in the World
chapters.summary: On her first time outside the South, Walter observes that African Americans in the little Washington town lived in a segregated neighborhood though children could attend an integrated school, and remembers seeing the Pacific Ocean for the first time, the roses of Portland, Oregon, and Mount St. Helens snow-capped beyond her back door.

chapters.title: Fifty Dollars a Month to Teach
chapters.summary: Walter explains that after the shipyards she would not work for the wages Louisiana paid, where a college graduate could only teach or do maintenance, and she turned down a Shreveport offer of fifty dollars a month to teach high school English and run the library, while her mother warned that college washes you down so you no longer fit the place.

chapters.title: Go See Mary
chapters.topic: A Healer Mother and Her Cures
chapters.summary: The youngest of seven and the first to attend college, Walter describes her mother, a midwife and healer of little formal education who cured pneumonia before penicillin with a tea made from moldy pig feed, treated kidney problems with red-hot oyster shells in cooled water, and to whom doctors sent patients they could not help, saying go see Mary.

chapters.title: A Woman on the Bus Named Mozelle Moore
chapters.summary: Walter recounts going to Los Angeles in 1944, staying first in a Skid Row hotel on Fifth Street, finding no YWCA for Black women, and meeting on a bus a friendly, intelligent woman she had been taught she could trust, Mozelle Moore, who took her in, introduced her to her sorority sisters, and set her on her way.

chapters.title: A California Credential
chapters.summary: She describes finding work right away in Los Angeles, where there was only one Black high school teacher and a California credential was required, so she worked first as a clerk in the classified section of the Los Angeles City Schools, returned to California State University, Los Angeles for her credential, and then began teaching elementary school.

chapters.title: You Don't Want to Get Involved With Him
chapters.summary: Walter tells of meeting her husband at a Methodist church, warned by a sorority sister that he had many girlfriends, of his lending her money for a hat and dress because he knew she would not want anything held over her, and of marrying in 1947 a man already active since 1942 in CORE and an anti-segregation organization called Drew.

chapters.title: Negotiating the Stores and Banks
chapters.summary: She describes CORE's growing work by 1958 around Central Avenue in Los Angeles, picketing and negotiating with stores and banks that hired Black people only as janitors, winning sales and boxboy jobs and management training in the grocery stores, and pressing the more reluctant banks until they hired tellers and trained people for management.

chapters.title: Testing the Law With Suitcases
chapters.summary: Walter recounts CORE's interracial work and its alliance with the ACLU, and how, after Jesse Unruh's 1959 bill outlawed segregated accommodations, members drove to motels with suitcases and children to be denied rooms a white couple would then receive, suing with a pro bono lawyer on the conviction that a law is worth little until it has been tested.

chapters.title: Frederick Douglass on the Wall
chapters.summary: Walter explains that acting against injustice came naturally from being taught to resist with dignity, that she began teaching in 1955 among many young African American teachers, and that as an active, demonstrating teacher who put Frederick Douglass on the wall beside Washington and Lincoln, she once overheard a vice principal tell a white pupil he need not stay at the school.

chapters.title: Beautiful Because Teacher Told Me So
chapters.summary: She describes wearing her hair natural since the 1950s, inspired by an African friend, when almost no one but the singer Abbey Lincoln did, her husband's pride in it, setting out brown paint so children could paint themselves, and the precious moment a girl called black in tears came days later to declare herself beautiful because her teacher told her so.

chapters.title: If You Write Them, I'll Publish Them
chapters.topic: From Lillie of Watts to Reading Rainbow
chapters.summary: Walter recounts how, wanting positive books for her children when so few existed, a Los Angeles publisher who issued her Lillie of Watts told her that if she wrote the books he would publish them, and how after many rejection slips a young Harvard graduate at Scholastic took her up, leading to editor Barbara Lalicki and Ty's One-Man Band, the very first book on Reading Rainbow.

chapters.title: My Purpose Was for Children to See Themselves
chapters.summary: She laments the near absence of Black editors in major publishing houses and of books by and for Black children, recalls refusing to let an illustrator make the boy in My Mama Needs Me white because her purpose was for children to see themselves, praises Ezra Jack Keats's The Snowy Day, and explains that every character in her books must make a choice, find courage, and so grow and change.

chapters.title: We Came to Take Our Seats
chapters.topic: Award Books and the Mississippi Challenge
chapters.summary: Walter tells of touring the nation reading to children and winning prizes, the Coretta Scott King Award for Justin and the Best Biscuits in the World, a Jane Addams Children's Book Award, a young-adult book on Elizabeth Mum Bett Freeman winning her freedom under the Massachusetts Constitution, and a book on the 1964 and 1968 Mississippi Challenge in which Fannie Lou Hamer told the Democratic National Convention they had not come to do nothing but to take their seats.

chapters.title: There Can Be No Peace Without Justice
chapters.summary: She describes her piece for The Big Book for Peace on the MFDP's wait outside Congress and the path to the Voting Rights Act of 1965, her worry that the right to vote is again in danger, and her hope that her books show children, by characters like the enslaved boy who insists on learning to read in Alec's Primer, that a good choice is one good for all living things, that it takes courage, and that courage brings growth.

chapters.title: I Rebutted His Review of Baldwin
chapters.topic: Reviewing for the Los Angeles Times
chapters.summary: Walter recounts reviewing books for the Los Angeles Times when funding opened for multicultural material, reading the works of Du Bois and Nelson Mandela's I Shall Not Be Moved, and rebutting a Times reviewer who called James Baldwin a bigot, proving from Baldwin's own closing words that he sought to save the nation and give it justice, after which she decided she would rather write books than criticize others' work.

chapters.title: A Lone Voice Sang Oh Freedom
chapters.topic: Nazis at a Carson Housing Picket
chapters.summary: She describes marching in Los Angeles housing tracts that would not sell to Black families, and a Saturday picket in present-day Carson where carloads of white men in Nazi uniforms and swastikas arrived with placards reading that ovens were too good for them, how the survivor Herbert who had been in a German concentration camp staggered from the line, and how a lone voice singing Oh Freedom lifted her anger and left her at peace.

chapters.title: They Said She's a White Woman
chapters.topic: A Festival in Nigeria and Ghana
chapters.summary: Walter recalls being invited as the only children's book writer with a group of artists and sculptors to the African Festival of Culture in Nigeria, then traveling to Ghana and Cameroon, where market women debated which ethnic group she belonged to until they heard her voice and exclaimed she was a white woman, stirring the same feeling as a soldier in her Louisiana youth calling out to come see a pretty Black girl.

chapters.title: The Door of No Return
chapters.summary: She recounts the Ghanaian poet Kofi Awoonor taking her to Elmina Castle, where a guide spoke of the captives without connection, and how standing by the door of no return and the guns trained on the slave ships, told her group could not carry the flag at the festival, she broke down and cried, realizing she was neither African nor American but no three-fifths of a person, her blackness not a lack but a unique wholeness.

chapters.title: From Guinea to Haiti to Louisiana
chapters.summary: Walter describes returning unable to speak of feeling unique for fear of seeming arrogant, then setting out to learn her heritage, tracing a grandmother from Guinea who was part French and reached Louisiana from Haiti after the Toussaint Louverture revolution, studying African and Christian religions, and seeing in the comparison why her people could turn the other cheek and walk the second mile.

chapters.title: Acknowledge the Pain on Both Sides
chapters.summary: She argues that the United States, like South Africa, needs a real discourse on race to be healed, that the Kerner Commission's call to confront racism was denied, and that the oppressed and the descendants of oppressors must sit together and acknowledge a shared pain of guilt and anger, citing relatives of oppressors too depressed to leave their houses on learning what their families had done.

chapters.title: Breaking Bread Breaks Barriers
chapters.summary: Walter recalls a peace march to Moscow where each town greeted the walkers with a round loaf of bread and salt, so that sharing the bread broke barriers and made strangers no longer the other, helping her understand at last why the South had forbidden eating together, and how, awarded the Coretta Scott King prize during the walk, she chose to keep walking for peace rather than attend the ceremony.

chapters.title: When You Know It, You Will Do It
chapters.summary: She affirms there can be no peace without justice, that the freedom struggle was the work of many young people and named leaders long before the 1960s, that real change comes from those who truly listen and recognize that all are one, and that, as Howard Thurman stressed, when you know a thing you will do it, the faith that keeps her bringing a message fit for the time.

chapters.title: All Things Being Equal
chapters.topic: Age, a Memoir, and Her Sons
chapters.summary: At ninety years old, Walter wishes she were twenty years younger and that she could publish a memoir though publishers now want celebrity stories, and acknowledges her two sons, one who died in 2008 after work in the Black Studies department at Cal State Los Angeles, the other in Santa Monica, and the grandson who brought her back to California and takes good care of her.

chapters.title: We Could Not Turn White, Red, or Blue
chapters.summary: Walter closes by recalling work in the 1970s, sponsored by the Western Interstate Commission for Higher Education under nurse Marie Branch, in which a group coined the term people of color rather than minorities and reformed nursing care, teaching that shock could not be read by red, white, and blue on dark skin, opening visiting hours and family rooms for transitions, and respecting cultural needs in food and water.

loc_video.caption: Mildred Pitts Walter oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in San Mateo, California, 2013 March 01
